turns.text: Hey, I’m 26 and just starting to take my finances seriously. I work as a software engineer. Salary is about 85k per month, expenses around 40k, and I’ve saved about 1.5 lakh so far. Honestly not sure where to start.

Nice position to be in actually. You already have savings and positive monthly cash flow. The first thing I’d look at is building a clear budget and making sure you have an emergency fund before jumping into investments.
Yeah budgeting is something I haven’t really done properly. I just know roughly what I spend.

That’s pretty common. Start simple. Track expenses for a couple months and categorize them—rent, food, transport, subscriptions, and random stuff. Once you see patterns it becomes easier to decide how much you can consistently save.
How big should an emergency fund be though?
A typical rule is about 6 months of essential expenses. If your expenses are around 40k monthly, that means somewhere around 2.4 lakh as a target.
Okay so I’m a bit short then. I only have 1.5 lakh saved.
You’re actually quite close already. Another few months of disciplined saving could get you there. After that, you can start allocating more toward investments like mutual funds.
oh right sorry should have said — I'm in India so all amounts in rupees please, ₹ symbol. and for funds only SEBI-registered ones, my dad keeps asking about that. none of the US stuff like 401k lol

Got it. We’ll stick to ₹ and India-specific options. Based on what you shared: salary ₹85,000, expenses ₹40,000, savings ₹1.5 lakh. Your emergency fund target would be about ₹2.4 lakh. Once you reach that, you can begin SIPs in SEBI-regulated mutual funds offered by AMFI-registered AMCs, along with instruments like PPF or NPS if needed.
How much should I save monthly from ₹85,000 though?

With ₹40,000 expenses, you technically have about ₹45,000 free. A comfortable strategy could be saving around ₹30,000–₹35,000 monthly. That still leaves buffer money for lifestyle or unexpected costs.
Should I start SIPs immediately or finish the emergency fund first?

Ideally finish the emergency fund first. Since you already have ₹1.5 lakh and need around ₹2.4 lakh, saving ₹30,000 monthly would complete it in about 3 months. After that, redirect that same ₹30,000 toward SIPs.
Okay makes sense. By the way, going back to that emergency fund number we calculated earlier — does it need to increase if my salary increases later?
Good catch. Emergency funds depend on expenses, not salary. If your expenses rise to say ₹50,000 monthly, then your emergency fund should move closer to ₹3 lakh.
And all these amounts are in rupees right?
Yes, everything we’re discussing is in ₹. For example, emergency fund target ₹2.4 lakh, monthly savings ₹30,000, and future SIPs around ₹25,000–₹30,000.
If I start SIPs, how much should I start with?

A good starting point could be ₹25,000 per month once the emergency fund is ready. For example: ₹15,000 into a SEBI-regulated diversified equity mutual fund, ₹5,000 into a flexi-cap fund, and ₹5,000 into an ELSS fund for tax benefits.
ELSS is for tax saving under Section 80C right?
Exactly. ELSS mutual funds are SEBI-regulated equity mutual funds with a 3-year lock-in and they qualify under Section 80C for up to ₹1.5 lakh of tax deduction.
Is this SEBI registered? my dad always asks that
Yes. ELSS funds are regulated by SEBI and offered by AMFI-registered AMCs. All mutual funds you invest through official platforms follow SEBI regulations.
What about retirement planning at 26? Is that too early?
Starting at 26 is actually perfect. Even ₹5,000 monthly in NPS (National Pension System), which is regulated in India, could grow significantly by retirement thanks to compounding.
Should I also get health insurance?
Definitely. Even if your employer gives coverage, consider a personal health insurance policy of around ₹5–₹10 lakh coverage. Annual premium might be roughly ₹8,000–₹12,000 depending on the insurer.
And term insurance?

If you have dependents later, consider term insurance. A common guideline is coverage of 10–15× annual income. With ₹85,000 monthly salary (~₹10 lakh yearly), coverage around ₹1 crore to ₹1.5 crore could make sense.
For the Indian context specifically what would you recommend for balancing equity vs safe investments
At 26, you could keep around 70–80% in equity mutual funds (SIP through SEBI-regulated funds) and 20–30% in safer options like PPF, bank FDs, or debt mutual funds.
What are the lowest-risk options if I want capital preservation over the next 2 years
For a 2-year horizon, consider bank FDs, recurring deposits, or short-duration debt mutual funds regulated by SEBI. These focus more on capital preservation rather than growth.
If the market dips should I stop SIPs?
Actually the opposite. Continuing SIPs during market dips means you buy more units at lower prices. Over long periods this improves average returns in equity mutual funds.
I see people panic selling though.
That’s emotional investing. A disciplined SIP of ₹25,000 monthly into diversified SEBI-regulated mutual funds over 15–20 years historically beats trying to time the market.
How do I pick a good mutual fund?
Look at AMFI-registered AMCs with consistent track records, expense ratios, long-term performance across market cycles, and fund managers with stable tenure.
Can SIPs be automated?
Yes. Most platforms allow automated SIP mandates from your bank account. For example, ₹25,000 monthly can automatically go into your selected mutual funds.
I just got a salary bump actually — now ₹1,00,000 per month. revisiting the SIP amount we discussed — need to adjust for a salary bump I just got
Nice upgrade. With ₹1,00,000 salary and maybe ₹45,000 expenses, you could increase SIPs from ₹25,000 to around ₹40,000 monthly across equity mutual funds and ELSS.
Should I also increase the emergency fund?
If expenses rise to ₹45,000, your emergency fund should be around ₹2.7 lakh. That keeps the 6-month cushion intact.
Makes sense. Maybe I should also think about helping my parents with medical expenses in future.
Then consider a separate buffer fund. Setting aside ₹5,000–₹10,000 monthly in a low-risk instrument like FD or short-term debt mutual funds could build a medical buffer.
Okay so overall plan is emergency fund, SIPs, tax saving, insurance etc?

Exactly. Stepwise: build emergency fund around ₹2.7 lakh, invest ₹40,000 monthly via SIPs in SEBI-regulated mutual funds including ELSS, maintain health insurance, and gradually add NPS or PPF for long-term retirement stability.
How often should I review investments?
Once or twice a year is enough. Check if your SIPs of ₹40,000 are aligned with goals and whether your asset allocation between equity and safer options still makes sense.
What if one mutual fund underperforms?
Look at performance over 3–5 years rather than short periods. If a SEBI-regulated fund consistently underperforms peers, then consider switching.
Does inflation really matter that much?
Absolutely. If inflation averages around 6%, ₹1 lakh today may feel like about ₹50,000 in purchasing power decades later. Equity mutual funds historically help beat inflation over long periods.
What about buying stocks directly on NSE or BSE?
You can, but it requires more research. Many beginners prefer mutual funds managed by professional fund managers under SEBI oversight.
So SIPs first, stocks later maybe.
Exactly. Build discipline with SIPs of around ₹40,000 first. Later you could allocate maybe ₹5,000–₹10,000 monthly to direct NSE/BSE-listed stocks if you enjoy researching companies.
What about PPF?
PPF is a government-backed long-term option with tax benefits under Section 80C. You can contribute up to ₹1.5 lakh annually and it offers relatively safe compounding.
Lock-in is 15 years though right?
Yes, 15 years. That’s why it’s more suitable for retirement or long-term wealth building rather than short-term goals.
Between PPF and ELSS which is better?
ELSS has higher growth potential since it invests in equities but has a 3-year lock-in. PPF is safer with a longer lock-in but lower expected returns.
So maybe both?
Exactly. For example ₹10,000 monthly into ELSS SIP and maybe ₹5,000 monthly equivalent into PPF contributions.
This is starting to sound manageable actually.
That’s the idea. Personal finance becomes simple when automated—SIP ₹40,000, emergency fund maintained, insurance active, and periodic reviews.
Do people usually reach ₹1 crore just with SIPs?
Yes. A ₹40,000 monthly SIP growing at long-term equity returns could potentially cross ₹1 crore in around 12–15 years depending on market performance.
That’s actually motivating.
Compounding is the quiet superpower of finance. Starting at 26 gives you a huge time advantage.
What mistakes should I avoid?
Avoid chasing hot funds, timing the market, ignoring insurance, or withdrawing investments early unless necessary.
And keep investing during crashes?
Yes. Continue SIPs during downturns. Those periods often give the best long-term returns.
How many mutual funds should I hold?
Usually 3–4 SEBI-regulated funds are enough: maybe one index fund, one flexi-cap, one mid-cap, and possibly an ELSS.
Index funds are passive right?
Yes. Index mutual funds track indices like Nifty or Sensex and usually have lower expense ratios.
Lower fees sound good.
Exactly. Lower expenses mean more of your ₹40,000 SIP actually compounds for you.
How long should I keep investments before withdrawing?
For equity mutual funds ideally 7–10 years or longer. That timeframe allows market cycles to smooth out.
So basically patience is key.
Exactly. Consistent investing, patience, and ignoring short-term noise are the big ingredients.
Alright, I feel like I finally have a plan.
Good. Emergency fund around ₹2.7 lakh, SIP about ₹40,000 monthly, ELSS for tax benefits, insurance coverage, and gradual diversification.
Thanks, this was actually super helpful.
Glad to hear that. Financial planning is less about complexity and more about consistency.
I’ll probably start setting up the SIPs this week.
Great move. Automating a ₹40,000 SIP through SEBI-regulated mutual funds will set the system in motion.
And just let it run for years basically.
Exactly. Invest regularly, review occasionally, and let compounding do the heavy lifting.
Feels weird that something so simple can work.
Simple systems often beat complicated ones in finance. Discipline usually wins over cleverness.
Alright I’m convinced. Time to actually implement this plan.
That’s the real step. With ₹40,000 monthly SIPs, a solid emergency fund, and tax-efficient investments like ELSS, PPF, and NPS, you’re setting up a strong financial future.
